nodes.title: Recognise an inequality and the four order symbols
nodes.brief: Identify a statement as an inequality when two numbers or expressions are related by one of the symbols <, >, less-than-or-equal, or greater-than-or-equal, distinguishing it from an equation.
nodes.quote: Two real numbers or two algebraic expressions related by the symbol  $(<), (>), (\leq)$  or  $(\geq)$  form an *inequality*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Four order symbols connect two quantities: <, >, <=, >=.
An inequality relates two numbers or expressions by one of the four order symbols: < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less than or equal), >= (greater than or equal). Unlike an equation, it has NO equals sign — 30x < 200 is an inequality, not an equation.

PART 2 — Translate words to the correct symbol.
'At most' means <=, 'at least' means >=, 'less than' means <, 'more than' means >. Many real situations (budgets, ranges) are naturally inequalities. Distinguish the strict symbols (<, >) from the inclusive ones (<=, >=).
keyMoves: Spot one of the four order symbols (<, >, less-or-equal, greater-or-equal) connecting two quantities
Distinguish an inequality from an equation by the absence of the equals sign
Translate a phrase like 'at most' or 'less than' into the correct order symbol
misconceptions: Treating an inequality as an equation because it relates two expressions
Confusing the strict symbols (<, >) with the inclusive ones (less-or-equal, greater-or-equal)

refreshers.trigger: "Greater than" / "less than" as an order on numbers — and how it behaves for negatives
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: Before we name the symbols, a quick gut check: which is the larger number, -3 or -2? And what does it actually mean for one number to be 'greater than' another?
ladder: If you owe ₹3 versus owe ₹2, which leaves you worse off — i.e. which is the smaller amount of money?
On a number line, which sits further to the LEFT: -3 or -2?
So between -3 and -2, which one is the greater number?
refreshers.answer: For any two real numbers, exactly one is larger: a is 'greater than' b (written a > b) when a lies to the RIGHT of b on the number line, and 'less than' (a < b) when it lies to the LEFT. For positive numbers this matches counting: 5 > 3. For negatives it can feel backwards — -2 > -3, because -2 is to the right of -3 (a debt of ₹2 is less severe than a debt of ₹3). This ordering is the whole foundation of inequalities: notice 3 > 2 but -3 < -2, which is exactly why multiplying an inequality by a negative number will later flip its direction.
refreshers.returnCue: So 'greater' just means further right on the number line, even for negatives. Now let's put names to the four symbols that record this ordering: <, >, ≤, ≥.

nodes.title: Define a solution and the solution set of an inequality
nodes.brief: State that a solution is any value of the variable that makes the inequality a true statement, and that the set of all such values is the solution set.
nodes.quote: any solution of an inequality in one variable is a value of the variable which makes it a true statement.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — A solution makes the inequality TRUE; the solution set is all such values.
A solution of an inequality is any value of the variable that makes it a true statement. The set of ALL such values is the solution set. Unlike an equation, an inequality usually has many solutions, not one.

PART 2 — The solution set depends on the number set (N, Z, or R).
Which values qualify depends on where the variable ranges. For 30x < 200 over whole numbers, the solution set is {0,1,2,3,4,5,6} (true up to x=6, false from x=7). Over the reals it would be x < 20/3. Always state the solution set relative to N, Z, or R.
keyMoves: Test a value by substituting it and checking whether the inequality is true
Collect all values that make it true into the solution set
State the solution set relative to the chosen number set (N, Z, or R)
misconceptions: Treating an inequality as having a single solution like an equation
Ignoring which number set the variable ranges over when listing the solution set

nodes.title: Apply the two rules for manipulating an inequality (reverse the sign on a negative)
nodes.brief: Add or subtract the same number on both sides freely, and multiply or divide both sides by a number - reversing the inequality sign whenever that number is negative.
nodes.quote: Both sides of an inequality can be multiplied (or divided) by the same positive number. But when both sides are multiplied or divided by a negative number, then the sign of inequality is *reversed*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Rule 1: add/subtract the same number on both sides (sign unchanged).
You may add or subtract the same quantity on both sides of an inequality without changing its direction. Example: x - 3 < 5 gives x < 8 by adding 3.

PART 2 — Rule 2: multiply/divide by a number, REVERSING the sign on a negative.
Multiplying or dividing both sides by a POSITIVE number keeps the sign. But by a NEGATIVE number, the inequality sign REVERSES (< becomes >). Example: -2x < 6 gives x > -3 (sign flips on dividing by -2). This reversal — evident from 3 > 2 but -3 < -2 — is the one feature distinguishing inequalities from equations. Forgetting it is the most common error.
keyMoves: Add or subtract the same quantity on both sides, keeping the sign the same (Rule 1)
Multiply or divide both sides by a positive number without changing the sign (Rule 2)
Reverse the inequality sign whenever you multiply or divide both sides by a negative number
misconceptions: Forgetting to reverse the inequality sign after multiplying or dividing by a negative number
Reversing the sign when adding or subtracting, or when multiplying by a positive number

nodes.title: Classify inequalities as numerical/literal/double and as strict or slack
nodes.brief: Sort inequalities into numerical, literal, and double, and separately into strict (<, >) versus slack (less-or-equal, greater-or-equal).
nodes.quote: 3 < 5; 7 > 5 are the examples of *numerical inequalities* while
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — By quantity: numerical, literal, or double.
Numerical inequalities relate plain numbers (3 < 5). Literal inequalities involve a variable (x < 5). Double inequalities bound a quantity on both sides (3 <= x < 5), which is read as TWO simultaneous conditions: x >= 3 AND x < 5.

PART 2 — By symbol: strict vs slack.
Strict inequalities use < or > and EXCLUDE the endpoint. Slack (inclusive) inequalities use <= or >= and INCLUDE the endpoint. This determines whether a boundary value belongs to the solution set — treating a slack inequality as strict (or vice versa) is a common error.
keyMoves: Label an inequality numerical, literal, or double by the quantities it relates
Read a double inequality as two simultaneous conditions on the variable
Distinguish strict (<, >) from slack (less-or-equal, greater-or-equal) by whether the endpoint is included
misconceptions: Treating a slack inequality as strict and excluding the boundary value
Misreading a double inequality as a single condition instead of two simultaneous ones

nodes.title: Identify a linear inequality in one or two variables
nodes.brief: Recognise ax + b (order) 0 as linear in one variable and ax + by (order) c as linear in two, and tell these apart from quadratic inequalities.
nodes.quote: Inequalities from (5) to (8) are *linear inequalities* in one variable x when  $a \neq 0$
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Linear means the variable appears only to the first power.
ax + b < 0 (or with >, <=, >=) is a linear inequality in one variable x, provided a != 0. ax + by < c is linear in two variables x, y, provided a, b are not both zero. The defining feature: no variable is raised to a power higher than 1.

PART 2 — A squared (or higher) term makes it non-linear.
An inequality with an x^2 term, like x^2 + 3x - 4 <= 0, is QUADRATIC, not linear. Calling such an inequality linear is the common error. This chapter covers only linear inequalities in one and two variables.
keyMoves: Match a one-variable inequality to the form ax + b (order) 0 with a not zero
Match a two-variable inequality to the form ax + by (order) c with a, b not zero
Reject any inequality with a squared (or higher) term as non-linear (quadratic)
misconceptions: Calling a quadratic inequality (with an x-squared term) linear
Forgetting the condition that the coefficient of the variable must be non-zero

nodes.title: Solve a linear inequality in one variable algebraically
nodes.brief: Use the two rules to isolate the variable in a linear inequality and write the solution set over the natural numbers, integers, or real numbers.
nodes.quote: Henceforth, unless stated otherwise, we shall solve the inequalities in this Chapter in the set of real numbers.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Collect variable terms on one side, constants on the other, then divide.
Use Rule 1 to gather the variable on one side and constants on the other, then Rule 2 to divide by the coefficient — REVERSING the sign if that coefficient is negative. Example: 5x - 3 < 3x + 1 gives 2x < 4, so x < 2. And 4x + 3 < 6x + 7 gives -2x < 4, so x > -2 (flip on dividing by -2).

PART 2 — Write the solution set as a list (N, Z) or an interval (R).
Over the reals, x < 2 is the interval (-inf, 2) and x >= 8 is [8, inf). Over naturals/integers it is a list of values. Unless stated otherwise, solve over the reals. Give the full solution set, not a single number.
keyMoves: Collect the variable terms on one side and the constants on the other using Rule 1
Divide by the coefficient using Rule 2, reversing the sign if that coefficient is negative
Write the solution set as a list (over N or Z) or as an interval (over R)
misconceptions: Failing to reverse the inequality when dividing by a negative coefficient
Giving a single number as the answer instead of the full solution set or interval

refreshers.trigger: "Interval notation" — round bracket ( ) excludes the endpoint, square bracket [ ] includes it, and -inf / inf mean unbounded, used to write a solution set over the reals like (-inf, 2) or [8, inf)
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we write the answer: when we write the solution as (-inf, 2), what are those round brackets telling us — and why a round bracket and not a square one?
ladder: if x < 2 means every real number strictly below 2, is the number 2 itself part of that set?
and if instead x <= 8, is 8 itself part of that set this time?
so for a number that is NOT included (like 2 above) versus one that IS included (like 8), which bracket would you reach for — round ( ) or square [ ]?
there's no biggest number below 2 — it just keeps going down forever. what symbol could stand in for 'no lower limit at all'?
refreshers.answer: An interval is just a stretch of the number line written compactly. A round bracket ( ) means the endpoint is EXCLUDED — so x < 2 is (-inf, 2), since 2 is not in the set. A square bracket [ ] means the endpoint is INCLUDED — so x >= 8 is [8, inf), since 8 is in the set. The symbol -inf (and inf) just means the set runs without bound in that direction, and infinity is never a reachable point, so it ALWAYS gets a round bracket. That's exactly the notation PART 2 of this node wants: a strict answer like x < 2 becomes (-inf, 2), and a slack one like x >= 8 becomes [8, inf).
refreshers.returnCue: so round = endpoint out, square = endpoint in, and infinity always round. now let's solve our inequality and write its solution set as one of these intervals over the reals.

nodes.title: Represent the solution of an inequality on a number line
nodes.brief: Graph a one-variable inequality's solution on a number line, using an open circle for strict and a dark circle for inclusive endpoints, with the line shaded left or right.
nodes.quote: To represent x < a (or x > a) on a number line, put a circle on the number a and dark line to the left (or right) of the number a.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Open circle for strict (<, >), filled circle for inclusive (<=, >=).
After solving, draw the solution on a number line. For a STRICT inequality (x < a or x > a), put an OPEN (hollow) circle at a to show a is excluded. For an INCLUSIVE inequality (x <= a or x >= a), put a FILLED (dark) circle at a to show a is included.

PART 2 — Shade left for less-than, right for greater-than.
Shade the number line to the LEFT of a for x < a or x <= a, and to the RIGHT for x > a or x >= a. Example: x < 3 is an open circle at 3 with shading to the left. The two common errors are wrong circle type and shading the wrong side.
keyMoves: Solve the inequality first to get the form x < a, x > a, or an inclusive version
Place an open circle at the endpoint for a strict inequality, a filled circle for an inclusive one
Shade the number line to the left for less-than and to the right for greater-than
misconceptions: Using a filled circle for a strict inequality (or an open circle for an inclusive one)
Shading the wrong side of the endpoint

refreshers.trigger: "Number line" — the picture of the real numbers we draw the solution on
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: We're about to draw the answer on a 'number line'. Quick check: if I draw a straight line and mark 0 in the middle, where do the negative numbers go, and which direction makes numbers bigger?
ladder: Picture a ruler or a thermometer laid flat — where is 0, and where is 1?
As you move to the RIGHT from 0, do the numbers get bigger or smaller?
So where would -2 sit relative to 0, and relative to +3?
refreshers.answer: A number line is a straight line on which every real number is a single point. We fix 0 somewhere, put the positive numbers to the right (1, 2, 3, …) and the negative numbers to the left (-1, -2, -3, …), equally spaced. Moving RIGHT means bigger, moving LEFT means smaller — so 3 is to the right of 1, and -2 is to the left of 0. Every real number, including fractions like 5/2, has exactly one spot. This is why we can show 'all x less than 3' as a shaded stretch of the line rather than listing numbers.
refreshers.returnCue: So the number line is just the reals drawn as points, bigger to the right. Now: on it, how do we mark whether the endpoint itself is included — open circle versus filled circle?

nodes.title: Translate a word problem into a linear inequality and solve it
nodes.brief: Set up a linear inequality from a statement problem (averages, ranges, mixtures), solve it, and interpret the result in context.
nodes.quote: Find the minimum marks he should get in the annual examination to have an average of at least 60 marks.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Name the unknown, translate the condition, solve.
Introduce a variable for the unknown. Translate the wording into an inequality, watching the key phrases: 'at least' -> >=, 'at most' -> <=, 'more than' -> >, 'less than' -> <. Then solve with the rules. Example: an average of at least 60 over three exams with marks 62 and 48 gives (62 + 48 + x)/3 >= 60, so x >= 70.

PART 2 — Interpret the answer in context.
State the result meaningfully: a minimum (>=), a maximum (<=), or an allowed range (a double inequality, e.g. 120 < x < 300 for an acid mixture). The two errors: mapping 'at least'/'at most' to strict signs, and stopping at the algebra without interpreting.
keyMoves: Introduce a variable for the unknown quantity
Translate each verbal condition ('at least', 'less than', etc.) into the correct inequality
Solve and interpret the result as a minimum, maximum, or allowed range
misconceptions: Mapping 'at least'/'at most' to strict signs instead of the inclusive ones
Stopping at the algebra without interpreting the answer in the problem's context

refreshers.trigger: "Average" (mean) of a set of marks — assumed in the exam-marks word problems
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: These problems hinge on an 'average of at least 60'. Quick check: if a student scores 62 and 48 in two tests, how do you work out their average, and what would the average of three tests be?
ladder: For two numbers, what do you do first — add them or divide them?
After adding 62 and 48 to get 110, what do you divide by — 2 or 3?
If a third mark x is added, what is the average of all three written as a formula?
refreshers.answer: The average (arithmetic mean) of a list of numbers is their SUM divided by HOW MANY there are. For 62 and 48: (62 + 48) / 2 = 55. For three marks 62, 48 and x it is (62 + 48 + x) / 3. So 'an average of at least 60 over three exams' becomes (62 + 48 + x)/3 ≥ 60 — multiply out by 3 to get 110 + x ≥ 180, then x ≥ 70. The key is dividing by the COUNT of items (here 3, the number of exams), not by 2.
refreshers.returnCue: So average = sum ÷ count, and 'at least 60' is the ≥ we built earlier. Let's set up the inequality for this marks problem and solve it.

nodes.title: Solve a double inequality by operating on all three parts
nodes.brief: Solve an inequality of the form p (order) expression (order) q by applying the rules to all three parts at once, reversing both signs on a negative multiplier.
nodes.quote: In this case, we have two inequalities,  $-8 \le 5x - 3$  and 5x - 3 < 7, which we will solve simultaneously.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Operate on ALL THREE parts at once.
A double inequality like -8 <= 5x - 3 < 7 is two conditions solved together. Apply each operation to all three parts simultaneously: add 3 throughout to get -5 <= 5x < 10, then divide by 5 to get -1 <= x < 2. The solution is the values satisfying both bounds.

PART 2 — On a negative multiplier, reverse BOTH signs and reorder.
When multiplying or dividing all three parts by a negative number, BOTH inequality signs reverse and the bounds swap order. Operating on only the middle and one end, or flipping only one sign, are the common errors.
keyMoves: Read the double inequality as two conditions that must hold simultaneously
Apply each operation (add, subtract, multiply, divide) to all three parts together
Reverse both signs and reorder the bounds when multiplying or dividing by a negative number
misconceptions: Operating on only the middle and one end instead of all three parts
Reversing only one of the two signs when multiplying or dividing by a negative number

nodes.title: Solve a system of linear inequalities and take the common solution
nodes.brief: Solve each inequality of a system separately, then find the values common to all of them, reading off the overlap on a number line.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Solve each inequality separately, then take the COMMON solution.
For a system of inequalities, solve each one on its own, then find the values satisfying ALL of them — the INTERSECTION of the separate solution sets. Example: 3x - 7 < 5 + x gives x < 6, and 11 - 5x <= 1 gives x >= 2; the common solution is 2 <= x < 6.

PART 2 — Use a number line to read off the overlap; intersection not union.
Draw each solution on a number line and read where the shadings OVERLAP — that overlap is the answer. The central error is taking the UNION (any one condition) instead of the INTERSECTION (all conditions); only the overlap counts.
keyMoves: Solve each inequality of the system separately
Mark each solution on the number line and find where they overlap
Report the intersection (the values satisfying every inequality) as the answer
misconceptions: Taking the union (any one condition) instead of the intersection (all conditions)
Mishandling the endpoints, e.g. including a value excluded by one of the inequalities

nodes.title: Solve and apply linear inequalities in one variable
nodes.brief: Bring the rules, algebraic solving, number-line representation, word-problem modelling, double inequalities, and systems together to solve and apply any linear inequality in one variable.
nodes.quote: In this Chapter, we shall confine ourselves to the study of linear inequalities in one and two variables only.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognise, then solve with the two rules.
The goal is to solve and apply any linear inequality in one variable. First recognise and classify it, then apply Rule 1 (add/subtract) and Rule 2 (multiply/divide, REVERSING the sign on a negative) to isolate the variable, and express the answer as a list (N, Z) or interval (R).

PART 2 — Represent, model, and extend to harder cases.
From the solution you can: represent it on a number line (open vs filled circle, shade the right side); model a word problem by translating wording ('at least' -> >=) into an inequality; solve a double inequality by operating on all three parts at once (both signs flip on a negative); and solve a system by INTERSECTING the separate solution sets. The recurring traps: forgetting the sign reversal, and confusing union with intersection.
keyMoves: Recognise and classify the inequality, then solve it with the two rules
Represent the solution on a number line and, for word problems, translate the wording into an inequality first
Handle double inequalities (all three parts) and systems (common solution) as extensions of the basic method
misconceptions: Forgetting the sign reversal on a negative multiplier in the harder cases (double inequalities, systems)
Confusing the union and intersection when combining several inequalities
